raw_recent_text: "Ugh, not again," you spout as the old wagon shutters, then dies.
Stranded on the road again, but this time, your money has gone dry.
All but your 50 dollar bill that Grandma gave you on her last Christmas.
You've been saving it for when you needed it most, but surely, it won't be enough.
The door creaks open, and you step out into the night sky, coughing up the smoke from your fried vehicle.
After pushing your car off the road and between the trees, there isn't much else left for you to do,
so you begin to wander down the dark, lonely street.
But at the end of the road, where concrete turned to stone turned to dirt, you notice a light up ahead, on the top of a hill.
As you waltz into the old, wooden shack, your eyes begin to light up with the fire of a thousand suns.
Roulette wheels! Poker tables! And in a dark corner of the abandoned casino, sits a dealer, shuffling cards for a new round of Blackjack.
That 50 dollars might just come in handy after all. Thanks, Grandma!
As you go to sit down at the table, you hear the Dealer cough, then watch as he sits up.
In a deep, and yet strained voice, the Dealer, cloaked in darkness, poses a question to you.
Would you like to play a game of Blackjack?
You have $50

raw_recent_text: ~ ~ ~ Day Summary ~ ~ ~
Superb!
You've survived 4 days!
Yesterday, at this time, you had $45.
Since then, you've managed to lose $11.
That brings you to a grand total of $34!
You're in the 'ramen noodle' tax bracket.
You might not be winning the race, but at least you're in the running, right? Sort of?
Don't forget what the bible taught you:
"Every tattoo is a temporary tattoo, because we are all slowly dying."
Press a key to continue: ~ ~ ~ Morning, Day 5 ~ ~ ~  True
You yawn, stretch, and feel genuinely alive. Not just surviving. Actually alive.
You stop by the convenience store for your usual run. The clerk behind the counter —
you've seen him before, skinny kid, maybe 19, name tag says KYLE — looks rough today.
His eyes are red. His hands are shaking when he scans your items. He drops your change twice.
"Sorry. Sorry about that. Long night."
He tries to smile but it comes out wrong. Like he forgot how smiles work.

Press a key to continue: ~ ~ ~ Morning, Day 7 ~ ~ ~  True
You wake up refreshed and optimistic. The optimism will probably wear off by noon, but for now, you bask in it.
You're leaning against your car, wincing. Everything hurts. Your body has been through
more punishment than it was designed for.
A man appears from behind the gas station. He's wearing a wrinkled white coat over a Hawaiian shirt.
He has a doctor's bag — the old-fashioned kind, black leather — and a smile that belongs
on a used car salesman.
"You look like a person in pain. I'm a person who fixes pain."
He opens his bag. Inside are rows and rows of pill bottles, neatly organized.
"Name's Dr. Feelgood. Not my real name, obviously. But nobody's using their real name ""out here, are they?"
He holds up a single white pill between his thumb and forefinger.
"First one's free. That's not a gimmick. I genuinely want to help. ""Think of it as a... demonstration."
You're in a car—your car—and water is pouring in through every crack.
You can't open the doors. The pressure is too much. The windows won't break.
The water reaches your waist. Your chest. Your neck. Your mouth.
You scream but water fills your lungs instead of air.
You wake up underwater.
THIS ISN'T A DREAM. Your car is IN THE RIVER. You must have rolled down the embankment while sleeping.
The water is at your waist. Rising fast. You fumble for the window crank.
It's stuck. You slam your elbow against the glass. Again. Again. AGAIN.
It cracks. Shatters. Water rushes in but you have an opening.
You squeeze through, cutting yourself on the glass, and kick toward the surface.
Air. Sweet, precious air. You drag yourself onto the bank and collapse.
By morning, the wagon is a drowned wreck-half-sunk, flooded through, and useless as transportation.
You can salvage your life, but not your ride. Most of what you kept inside is ruined or swept away. But you're alive.
Your mind feels... foggy. Something isn't right.

take a moment to reflect on this:
"let's embark on a journey, a meandering path not unlike the serpentine wanderings of a leaf caught in a capricious autumn breeze, which, as it dances to the silent music of nature, reminds us of the unpredictable choreography of existence. this leaf, let's call it gerald, flutters with an elegance borne of happenstance, a fragile vessel for the whims of the wind. gerald's journey is not linear, nor is it bound by the rigid expectations of society's ceaselessly grinding gears. instead, gerald twirls, dips, and soars, embracing the chaos with a grace we can only aspire to. now, consider the ant, industrious and steadfast, a creature of purpose and communal toil. our ant, whom we'll name beatrice, marches diligently on her quest for sustenance, her life a testament to the virtues of hard work and persistence. yet, in the grand tapestry of the cosmos, what is beatrice but a speck, a mere blip on the infinite canvas of the universe? and yet, does her insignificance in the face of the vast unknown render her efforts moot? i posit that it does not, for in the grand scheme, all actions, large and small, contribute to the intricate mosaic of existence. but back to gerald, who by now has traversed the convoluted landscapes of our imagination, touching upon the existential questions that haunt the periphery of our consciousness. what can we learn from gerald and beatrice? is it their resilience, their unyielding will to persist in the face of the insurmountable odds stacked against them by the very nature of their existence? or is it, perhaps, the simple beauty of their dance, a reminder that life, in all its complexity and confusion, offers moments of sublime beauty, fleeting and precious, to be cherished and remembered? as gerald finally comes to rest upon the earth, joining beatrice in the eternal cycle of life and death, we are reminded that all journeys, whether they be of leaves or ants or the human heart, are interconnected in the grand, bewildering dance of the cosmos. so, what was the point of this story, you may ask? well, that, my friend, is entirely up to you."

Take a moment to reflect on this:

"Let's embark on a journey, a meandering path not unlike the serpentine wanderings of a leaf caught in a capricious autumn breeze, which, as it dances to the silent music of nature, reminds us of the unpredictable choreography of existence. This leaf, let's call it Gerald, flutters with an elegance borne of happenstance, a fragile vessel for the whims of the wind. Gerald's journey is not linear, nor is it bound by the rigid expectations of society's ceaselessly grinding gears. Instead, Gerald twirls, dips, and soars, embracing the chaos with a grace we can only aspire to. Now, consider the ant, industrious and steadfast, a creature of purpose and communal toil. Our ant, whom we'll name Beatrice, marches diligently on her quest for sustenance, her life a testament to the virtues of hard work and persistence. Yet, in the grand tapestry of the cosmos, what is Beatrice but a speck, a mere blip on the infinite canvas of the universe? And yet, does her insignificance in the face of the vast unknown render her efforts moot? I posit that it does not, for in the grand scheme, all actions, large and small, contribute to the intricate mosaic of existence. But back to Gerald, who by now has traversed the convoluted landscapes of our imagination, touching upon the existential questions that haunt the periphery of our consciousness. What can we learn from Gerald and Beatrice? Is it their resilience, their unyielding will to persist in the face of the insurmountable odds stacked against them by the very nature of their existence? Or is it, perhaps, the simple beauty of their dance, a reminder that life, in all its complexity and confusion, offers moments of sublime beauty, fleeting and precious, to be cherished and remembered? As Gerald finally comes to rest upon the earth, joining Beatrice in the eternal cycle of life and death, we are reminded that all journeys, whether they be of leaves or ants or the human heart, are interconnected in the grand, bewildering dance of the cosmos. So, what was the point of this story, you may ask? Well, that, my friend, is entirely up to you."

After wandering from your vehicle, you find yourself deep in the woods.
Squirrels run by and up into the trees. The sun hits every branch and casts a shadow below.
And you wander on a natural path, journeying into the unknown.
As you walk along the path, you find a mother deer, with two children, walking the path towards you.
As you get closer, the mother appears cautious, but then runs in your direction, before stopping before you.
Her two children follow behind, and before you know it, the three of them wait in front of you.
You put your hand out, and pet the mother deer. She makes a happy squeak noise, and wags her tail.

decision_requests.raw_prompt_text: Do you accept the shadow's offer?
raw_recent_text: You might not be winning the race, but at least you're in the running, right? Sort of?
As my doctor was putting me under, he whispered into my ear:
"There was a safety meeting at work today. They asked me, 'What steps would you take in the event of a fire?' 'Fucking big ones' was the wrong answer."
Press a key to continue: ~ ~ ~ Morning, Day 33 ~ ~ ~  True
Your current sanity: 41%
You wake up with energy. Like, real energy. Not 'I didn't die in my sleep' energy.
You've been fighting sleep for days. Coffee. Slapping yourself. Driving with the windows down.
It doesn't matter. It comes when it wants to.
This time, you don't even realize you've fallen asleep. One moment you're driving.
The next, you're parked somewhere you don't recognize, engine off, and you can't move.
The shadow fills the entire car. It's everywhere. In the mirrors, in the corners,
in the gaps between the seats.
"I'm not what you think I am."
"I'm not a monster. I'm a door. Walk through me and the pain stops. The cold stops. The hunger, the loneliness, the endless, stupid cards — all of it. Gone."
"I've been watching you lose for so long. I'm offering you the only guaranteed win."
The cold is so intense your teeth are chattering. Or trying to — your jaw is locked.
Inside your head, you hear the question clearly:
"Do you accept?"
No.
Not a thought. A word. Your lips move even though your body is frozen.
You force it out through locked teeth and a locked jaw.
"No."
The shadow recoils. The cold retreats. For the first time,
you hear something from it that isn't confidence.
"...Interesting."
Your body releases. You sit up. The car is normal. Warm. Silent.
But in the rearview mirror, just for a second, you see it retreat into the shadows
behind your headrest. And you could swear it's smiling.
Your leg is broken. It just is. Has to be.
Your wagon isn't road-ready, but there are still places you can reach before sundown.
Would you like to spend your day driving somewhere?
1. Doctor's Office
2. Stay Home

You find yourself on a bridge. You don't remember leaving your car.

A voice in your head says: It would be so easy. Just let go.
With shaking hands, you pull out your phone. 988. The suicide hotline.
A voice answers. Calm. Kind. They talk to you for an hour.
They don't judge. They don't tell you you're being stupid. They just... listen.
When you finally hang up, you're sitting in your car. Still on the bridge. But back from the edge.
You're not fixed. But you're here. That matters.
A sense of clarity washes over you. The fog lifts, if only a little.

You wake up feeling like a million bucks. Well, maybe a few thousand bucks. But still great.
The sound of a tractor barrels closer. As you jump up from your seat, you see the tractor getting closer to your wagon.
The tractor drives beside your vehicle, and pushes right up against you, grinding the paint off your car. That's just mean.
You look up at the driver to see a beautiful black and white cow. Good god, it's Betsy. Why, Betsy, why.
The cow moos at you aggressively, and you roll down the window.
Betsy stares into your soul, then looks over at the seat next to you. It appears Betsy is interested in your pile of money.
Do you feed Betsy?
Her gray hair covers her glasses, and her hand trembles as she hands you a pen and a clipboard with some paperwork.
Of course it's paperwork.
After filling out your information, you walk back to the front desk, and hand the lady the clipboard. She smiles, and begins to speak to you.
I see you're here for a checkup. The Doctor will see you now.
Hey there champ! How are you? Doing all right? Let's check you out and make sure you're all up to snuff.
Why, you don't seem to really need my help. You appear a little worse for wear, but this medicine should do the trick.
You walk back to the front desk to checkout.
That will be $11

Your current sanity: 67%
You actually feel rested. Like, genuinely rested. This is what sleeping is supposed to feel like?
You're looking for a dropped quarter under the driver's seat when your hand
touches something cold and hard. Metal.
You pull it out. It's a lockbox. A real, proper lockbox. Steel. Heavy. About the size
of a shoebox. There's a keyhole on the front and a combination dial on top.
It's padlocked shut with a serious-looking Master lock.
You shake it. Something slides around inside. Multiple somethings.
They clink against each other like glass or metal.
This wasn't here yesterday. You'd have noticed. Which means someone put it
in your car while you were sleeping.
On the bottom, scratched into the metal with something sharp:
"DON'T OPEN UNTIL READY"

After wandering from your vehicle, you find yourself deep in the woods.
Squirrels run by and up into the trees. The sun hits every branch and casts a shadow below.
And you wander on a natural path, journeying into the unknown.
As you walk along the path, you find a mother deer, with two children, walking the path towards you.
As you get closer, the mother appears cautious, but then runs in your direction, before stopping before you.
Her two children follow behind, and before you know it, the three of them wait in front of you.
You put your hand out, and pet the mother deer. She makes a happy squeak noise, and wags her tail.
She touches her head to yours, then continues down the path, with her two children following.
Eventually, you get to the end of the path, and find the main road. You follow it back to your wagon, and take a seat, to rest for a moment.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
You feel anxious tonight.
Nightfall again, huh? Well, you know what's next.

Confused, and dazed, you sit up. As you open the door and get out of your car,
you notice a man, in a bright red suit, peering into your trunk.
The man sees you, and walks up to you.
"You. You're awake. Good. You know that you aren't supposed to be here? "
"This isn't a spot for people to live. This is a road for people to drive. I hope you know this."
"Do you know this?"
"This isn't a spot for people to live. This is a road for people to drive. I hope you know this."
"Do you know this?"
"You don't know this? How don't you know this? It's super obvious stuff, man. "
"People don't live at places where they're not supposed to, and that's exactly what you're doing right now. "
"I'd suggest you stop it, right this instant."
After the man tells you this, he looks up, and stares at the sun. And after about 20 seconds, he rubs his eyes, walks back to his car, and drives off.

Officer Martinez's cruiser pulls up next to your car. But this time, he's not in uniform.
He's wearing a flannel shirt and jeans. He's holding two cups of coffee.
He knocks gently this time.
"Hey. It's Martinez. Off duty. Thought you might want one of these."
He holds up a coffee cup.
He holds up a coffee cup.
"Alright, fair enough. Can't trust a cop, even off duty. I get it."
He sets the second coffee on your hood.
"It's here if you change your mind. It'll get cold, but hey. Cold coffee's still coffee."
He drives off. The coffee sits on your hood for an hour before you drink it. It's terrible.
But you drink every drop.

You wake up refreshed and optimistic. The optimism will probably wear off by noon, but for now, you bask in it.
You're at the gas station filling up — or rather, pretending to while you use the bathroom —
when you notice a man sitting at the bus stop across the street.
No striped shirt. No white paint. Just a regular guy in a brown jacket.
But you recognize the way he sits. The way he holds his hands. The way he watches everything.
That's the mime. That's Gerald.
You walk over. He sees you coming and tenses up, like he might run.
"Please. Sit down. Don't make it obvious."
His voice is quiet. Raspy. Like he hasn't used it in a long time.
"I was a performer. A real one. Theaters, festivals, the whole thing. "
"Then I saw something I shouldn't have. Backstage at a casino. Money changing hands. "
"Important hands. The kind of hands that make people disappear."
"The mime thing started as a disguise. Turns out, nobody looks twice at a mime. "
"You're invisible. The perfect hiding spot is where everyone can see you."
He glances around nervously.
"I've been watching you because you're in their territory. The gambling road. "
"They don't like people winning too much. Drawing attention."
"You're invisible. The perfect hiding spot is where everyone can see you."
He glances around nervously.
"I've been watching you because you're in their territory. The gambling road. "
"They don't like people winning too much. Drawing attention."
Gerald nods approvingly.
"Smart. The less you know, the safer you are. Just... be careful."
He walks away. No moonwalk this time. Just a scared man in a brown jacket.
It's so hard to even sit up straight. That's not normal.
Your wagon isn't road-ready, but there are still places you can reach before sundown.
Would you like to spend your day driving somewhere?
1. Doctor's Office
2. Stay Home
Choose a number:
In the dream, you walk into a bank. A real bank. With your name on the door.
The teller asks how much you'd like to deposit. You say "All of it." She doesn't blink.
You sign papers. Buy a house. A car that isn't also your bedroom. You eat at a restaurant with cloth napkins.
Then you wake up. The steering wheel is digging into your ribs. But the smile lingers.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
Another night. Another dance with chance.

"Car accident. Both of them. I was at the school grading papers when I got the call. ""The hospital Maria worked at was the one they were taken to. ""Her own colleagues..."
He can't finish that sentence. He doesn't need to.

"I left everything. The house, the job, the life. Started driving. ""I knew cards — I'd been running poker night for the faculty for years. ""Turns out, when you understand probability, you make a decent dealer."
"This road. Your car. These cards. It's all I have left. ""And the photo. I keep the photo."
He picks up the photograph and holds it for a moment before putting it back.
"You didn't ask. I appreciate that. But I needed to tell someone. ""Before I forget how to say their names out loud."
He deals the next hand. The cards feel different in his hands. Lighter.
Your leg is broken. It just is. Has to be.
Your wagon isn't road-ready, but there are still places you can reach before sundown.

Rain on the roof. Not a storm — just rain. The soft kind. The kind that taps a lullaby on the metal above you.
For the first time in weeks, your brain shuts up. No calculations. No strategies. No what-ifs.
Just you, the rain, and eight uninterrupted hours.
You wake up and for three seconds — three beautiful seconds — you forget where you are.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
The green felt remembers you. So do I.

decision_requests.raw_prompt_text: Ask Edgar how many of these 'just in case' graves he's dug?
raw_recent_text: Your efforts haven't gone unnoticed. They're just not always mentioned.
If aliens could talk, they'd probably say:
"Why go the extra mile when you can just complain about the first one? After all, life is not about the journey or the destination; it's about finding a good parking spot."
Press a key to continue: ~ ~ ~ Morning, Day 53 ~ ~ ~  True
You wake up and actually want to get out of the car. This is a historic moment.
Edgar is waiting for you at the gate. He has a thermos of coffee and two mugs.
"I want to show you something. You're going to want that coffee first."
He leads you to the back of the cemetery. The old section. The headstones here
are pre-Civil War. Crumbling. Names barely visible.
Past the old section, past the tool shed, past the compost pile,
there's a corner you haven't noticed before.
It's empty. No headstones. No markers. Just fresh-turned earth.
And one hole, freshly dug, with no name.
"That one's yours."
You stop. Coffee cup frozen halfway to your mouth.
"Not literally. Not yet. But I dug it the day I met you. ""I dig one for every drifter who passes through. Just in case. ""So nobody ends up in an unmarked grave somewhere."
"If you die on this road, I want you to have a proper resting place. ""With your name on it. And flowers, when I can find 'em."
He's serious. Dead serious. (Unfortunate phrasing, given the location.)
You don't ask. Some numbers are too heavy to carry.
"I'm not trying to scare you. I'm trying to give you a reason to NOT end up here. ""Every morning you wake up and that hole stays empty, that's a win."
You look at the empty grave. YOUR empty grave. And for some reason,
it doesn't scare you. It motivates you.
It's so hard to even sit up straight. That's not normal.
Your wagon isn't road-ready, but there are still places you can reach before sundown.
Would you like to spend your day driving somewhere?

2. Stay Home

Why, you don't seem to really need my help. You appear a little worse for wear, but this medicine should do the trick.
You walk back to the front desk to checkout.
